sentences.text: They were extremely drastic.
If-- if you can imagine I'm playing with the children, the Bulgarian children.
And all of a sudden, they begin to belong to an organization.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH].
They had to wear like a uniform, like a military uniform.
And they took extreme pride in that.
And those friends that I was with them, they would-- they would shy away from me.
Well, there are several of them actually that they didn't.
They would go home and they change the uniform.
We still go and play.
But you could see that it was totally different.
There was not that camaraderie, that closeness as before.
And that was the extreme, the ones that they were really tolerant.

But most of them became overnight, if you might say, so enamored with that Fascist idea of wearing uniforms and taking care of-- of the nation by getting rid of all this Jews and by-- by-- by restricting the Jews even though there were quite a few that they were really very reluctant to admit to all that, but they very openly things changed so much for us that, like I said, the first thing with my father, then my uncle that he was no longer a chairman of the board of Bulgarian bank.
They-- they dismissed him.
He had to resign.
And then my-- my other uncle lost all his properties.
They confiscated everything.
They lived in a beautiful home and they had to move to a much, much smaller area.
Lucky they still live there.

Then they were interned, because some of the most prominent Jews in the community were interned for a certain amount of time, being that they thought they were going to be shipped out to the camp, but they were not.
No.
There was not a ghetto.
They were taken from different cities.
And they were put in a place called Somovit right by the Danube.
That was a detention camp, not-- and they stayed there for six, seven months.
They-- they let them go home.
And it was so depressing all this.
But interesting thing how my mother and my father, I would hear them, they always had the faith in the Bulgarian people, in the clergy, in the king, that that's not going to happen.
They're not going to allow it to happen.
Rumors began to fly that the Nazis were advancing.
And all the sudden, we hear that Bulgarians signed a treaty with Germany, which in my opinion was the smartest thing the king ever did.
And that's one of the reasons we are here.
Rather the fighting, which like the King Peter of-- of-- of Yugoslavia did, or-- or Romania, when they were overtaken overnight, he decided to be their ally.
So what they did, instead of conquering them, he became an ally, so even though the Germans were occupying Bulgaria, we still had the movement as Jews right under the Nazi occupation.
We were not being restricted to the point where-- where-- where-- my father one day was attacked.
One night, they thought he was somebody else and they broke his nose.
And there was a rumor in the city that he was dead.
Well, he was not dead.
Until his last day, he would not have an operation for it.
But he had obstruction here.
They had broken his-- and he was a very handsome man.
[SIGHS] We continued to go to school.
But after that, the-- the Hebrew school was closed.
They confiscated the Hebrew school.
They confiscated the area of the synagogue.
We had our own-- our own Jewish community center.
They confiscated that.
The Maccabi salon, the Maccabi gym, which was a beautiful gym, brand new, and it was right in the area of-- of the school, was confiscated by the Fascists.
We were not able to pray the synagogue.
[SIGHS] And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] luckily, that was the first year.
You know, interesting thing, though, they allow us to go to Bulgarian schools, not to Hebrew schools.
I was able to go to a Bulgarian school and to go to high school.
And I was the only Bulgarian-- I was the only Jew amongst 55 Bulgarians.
I don't know, but it was something with me as a kid, I always had a tolerance for religion.
So during the classes, our home teacher would teach religion.
In the religion, all the Jewish kids were excused.
They could leave.
I never excused myself.
I stayed there.
In fact, when my time came to say the prayer, I said the-- the Bulgarian prayer, recited it.
But instead of crossing myself I had my-- my hands over my scrolls like this.
So I never crossed myself, but he was so impressed with that, was our home teacher, that came to our home.
He found out that my mother was a former teacher.

He had a very great discussion here, and also asked her if I will be helpful to certain-- there were peasant children that were sent to high school there and they were having little difficult time, so I could help them in-- with their study.
And I did.
So that kind of-- of freedom we did enjoy in school, but were not allowed any Jewish activities.
No.
No.
In America, I was, but not in Bulgaria.
[LAUGHS] No.
No.
That was absolutely something which was not done.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Intermarriage was absolutely to-- to a minimum there.
Very, very few people intermarried.
And they intermarried, they were not shunned by the community.
They were accepted, but it was very frowned upon.
I'd like to give you the picture of the contrast with the childhood, like I said, the-- the childhood of innocence that we can roam around all over the city.
And I remember as children we'd go to the train station to watch the Orient Express pass through.
You know, we were eight, nine years old.
Try to let my grandmother-- I mean, my grandchild to go now across the street at eight or nine.
Here we go from one side of the city, we walk all the way the side to the train station to watch the Conventional, to watch the Orient Express in its splendor pass through the city.
It was incredible.
And all of a sudden, you can't do any of this.
We would go all over, roam all over the hills, the beautiful hills in Plovdiv.
We can't go there.
All of a sudden, I can't be a Boy Scout.
All of a sudden, my father is out of work.
All of a sudden, we begin to hear the Jews are being persecuted.
The Jews are being sent to camps.
The Jews-- the words-- the words of the Holocaust begin to filter through.
It was the time of apprehension.
It was the time of-- of the anticipation of some real, real bad things to happen.
And they did.
They did.
All of a sudden, we begin to hear the news that the Jews are going to be assembled.
They're going to be sent.
They begin to-- we begin to get-- we began to hear the trains are being assembled.
And I'm talking now from a perspective of the child.
If you've seen the books that you read, you see that there was a delegation that went to the king.
And they formed from the intelligentsia.
The clergy with-- God save their soul-- Metropolit Stephan and Metropolit Kirill went to the king on our behalf.
I didn't know any of this.
All I knew I'm hearing all from my parents, we're doomed.
We're ready to be shipped out.
And the night came that we're-- we're really assembled by the police.
And they came and took us.
And they put us together in the school yard.
And we're waiting to be shipped out.
And my mother at that time said, it is not going to happen.
And that's the time after that we found out that the clergy went and pleaded for our lives in our behalf.

And I had promised my mother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sorry-- the day would come after it was all over that I will go back and pray at the graves of these two-- these great clergymen, these bishops that really put themselves on the line, which is unheard of anywhere in the world.
And I just went back there and I paid all my respects.
But those days, as we were assembled, I did not know that.
But in my meeting with the bishop, the very recent meeting, he told us that the bishop came to the churchyard, got up on a bench, and at the top of his voice said, children, don't worry.
You will be saved.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING]
Well, a few hours later, the-- the head of the police, the chief of police came over.
And we were assembled.
He went on the steps of the-- of the school.
That's where we were assembled.
And he said, Jews, go home.
Your lives have been spared.
[CRYING]
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yes.

What happened was in-- in Bulgaria during the war, through an edict of the king, they conscripted all male Jews from age of 19 or 20 to age of 55 or 50, I don't remember exactly, they're conscripted into the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was under paramilitary organization, where they would go-- they were wearing uniforms, really.
They were called [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].
And they would go and build roads and build bridges.

And under that-- under that pretext, the king was able, whenever the Jews were asked to be sent to the camps, when the Germans would insist on asking to be sent to the camps-- now, this is told to me by my mother and this is told to me by my father.
I have no firsthand knowledge, only-- that's the only knowledge I have, and-- which I verified everything after that with-- with the clergy on my trip recently.
But what they did is they-- they-- they dispersed the Jews from Sofia, which were 25,000, all over to the smaller cities.
And there, they conscripted the males so they can build bridges and they can build roads.
So the excuse of the king was always, well, I need the Jews to build roads.
And those roads are being built for you, for the Nazis, so they can go through Bulgaria quicker.
And the saddest thing that happened during that time, a lot of the-- those males have come-- have come from Greece, actually from Thrace and Macedonia.
And now those males were conscripted there.
And they were working with the rest of the Jews from Bulgaria.
Now, those were territories which were annexed-- were given to the king as a present from the Nazis, because he did not fight the Nazis, because he became their ally.
I'm not going to get into any controversy, but what it is that he did not have jurisdiction over those areas.
The fact that he had jurisdiction over us, I'm here.
Unfortunately, it's a very bad stain on the Bulgarian people, and they admit it themselves, that the Jews from Thrace and from Macedonia were shipped out to the camps.
And as they were going with the trains through the camps, many areas the same men who were part of their families, they saw their families depart to the camps.
Those, after they were sent back home-- after they completed their work over the winters, they would be sent home.
All of a sudden I see different young, handsome men, young men, strong men, they would be speaking Greek.
And eventually, they began to speak Bulgarian.
And they would speak Greek and Ladino.

So they were taken by our community and they were put in different homes, and whoever had more room-- right across the street from me there were two of those fellows that were housed in that area, because they had larger quarters than we did.
And we were slated to take one too, like I said.
But-- but those were people, like I said, from the Greek origin in Macedonia and Thrace, that they were spared because they were conscripted as-- Laborers
Laborers.
But their families were not spared. 13,000, I believe, they were gone.
Those are the-- the conscripts, like I said, only the male laborers.
Yeah.
They were--
And those Jews were not spared.
They were spared.
The laborers were spared.
Their families were not.
No.
[DRINKS]
They witnessed a lot of those families going through the areas where they were working.
Yes.
Yes.
They stayed there with us until most of them after that they immigrated to Israel.
As you know, the entire Jewish population was let-- was allowed to immigrate to Israel during the Communist regime.
But I left Bulgaria as a child, as we'll come to that, that-- with the Youth Aliyah.

But those-- we begin to witness all these young people, and there are quite a few of them that-- but they were very well integrated immediately into the community, because Ladino was a common language and they spoke Ladino.
And they also had to learn very quickly Bulgarian, because they worked under the Bulgarian officers, so they learned rather quickly.
The bishop did not say that.
The-- the police-- the chief of police said that.
Yeah.
He received the edict from whatever.
We don't know from where.
My mother-- I can't say that it was only the bishop.
But he played tremendous part of it.
My mother always mentioned their-- their names of Kirill and Stephan which such a reverence.
And she always broke down crying.
So that's the way I knew it.
I had no firsthand knowledge.
But I knew it from her and I knew through my father.
I knew it from our whole family.
They were held in such a high esteem.
I'll tell you a quick story.
When-- when he became Patriarch of-- of Bulgaria, Kirill, he went to visit Israel.
And the Jews came to-- to receive him, the Jews from Bulgaria.
Everyone at home took a piece of rug, whatever they had, and they laid it in front of him so he would not walk on the earth.
He would be walk-- in appreciation, in-- in-- in gratitude.
Now, so they did that as-- as a uniform gesture.
He was so revered.
He was so respected.
He was so loved by them, that that's the way they show their respect for him.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tremendous, tremendous influence they had in our lives.
And I was lucky to go back a month ago and pay my respects.
[SIGHS] Well--
Since those bad winds began to come, immediately there was a very big movement to try to leave Bulgaria.
I don't know the exact-- the exact way how it was done.
I can give it to you only from my perspective as a child at that time.
Oh, after we went back our home.
We went to our-- back to our home.
The plans were made.
My father said, you leave as quickly as you can.
Leave.

And immediately, we tried to start getting papers, because there was a movement at that time through the Jewish organizations, the world Jewry, that children-- there was a movement which was called the Youth Aliyah.
Henrietta Szold from Germany organized that.
She started that.

And with a Jewish agency, with the Jewish bodies, they were able to negotiate through the British somehow to-- were able to send the children from the age-- I'm giving you from my perspective as a child at that time.
So the minute that was made available, we began-- we registered.
We started taking out papers.
And there was a list of-- of children that-- and they start sending those children group by group.
So I was slated in a group of 42, which it came just shortly before the-- the Nazis had left.
I was slated to leave during the Nazis.
And everybody was able to leave during the Nazis.
Now remember, those were not smuggled.
Those were given official passports, papers, British visas.

And we are put on a train by ourselves, 42 children, after we have achieved all this status, to get the papers, to be registered, to-- to-- to have all the documentation approved that we didn't own money, all this.
And we're put on a train.

And the last thing I remember-- after we made all these preparations very anxiously and my father and the whole family was very, very anxious for-- for me to do that-- unfortunately, I couldn't take my kid brother with me.
That was a-- but many of our friends.
So we were a group of 42 children that we went from-- from Bulgaria, on the train.
And the last thing I remember, my mother running alongside the train-- waving goodbye.
But I would say this, that we were lucky, because five years later, the entire family came to Israel.
Like I said, like close to 46,000, 44,000 Jews were allowed to leave Bulgaria willingly.
They were not expelled.
They were not forced.
And that was one thing I don't know the mechanics how it was done, but the Communists allowed them to leave.
And now the ones that remained there were people who wanted to stay there.
So that was-- it was very difficult to leave the family.
And the most difficult thing for me was to leave my kid brother.
I mean, no question about my mother and my father.
But we were so closely and so attached to it.
And they came to the station.
And of course-- and I-- I can't forget that, my mother running alongside the train.
I mean, until today when there is a scene with- with trains, with stations, I always get very shook up.
It was-- But then also we were-- we had a lot of-- a lot of joy in that.
We were now embarking on a tremendous adventure.
We're children.
Imagine, I was not even 14.
I celebrated my 14th birthday in Israel.
And those children were my playmates or my-- my classmates.
And we're all together and looking forward to a new life.
Yes.
There were two adults who were put in the train that they were able to leave with us.
That was the entire supervision.
And I don't know the whole way.
They were like supervisors.
But they were also granted visas and papers and all this.
And and I think one of-- one of them was able to leave very quickly because he was with Turkish citizens some kind of way.
But there were two male supervisors that-- that oversaw this group of about 42 children.
[SIGHS] It took us like almost 24 hours to get to Istanbul, because I don't know the exact amount of time that-- from Plovdiv to Istanbul.
But also we stayed at the-- at the boundary.
We-- they kept us there for quite a few hours until they cleared papers and all this.
And then when we arrived to Istanbul, we met with the representative of the Jewish community.
Immediately they ushered us into a pensione on the Asian side.

And we were sequestered there for five days, until-- they waited until the train that came to take us from Istanbul through Turkey, through the Steppes of Turkey, through Anatol, all the way through Syria.
And I remember stopping in-- excuse me-- stopping in Aleppo, Syria, in which British soldiers gave us tea.
And that was amazing.
I mean, they were just getting from enriching and giving us tea.
And through Aleppo, we arrived-- the train ride from Istanbul to-- to Beirut was five or six days.
I don't remember exactly.
But it was a-- it was almost a week.
Then we arrived in Beirut.
Then we were met by the-- some representative of the Jewish community there too.
They took us by bus to-- to a hotel.
We stayed overnight there.
And the next day, we boarded buses and were taken to Palestine, like a detention-- British detention camp.
But it was not detention.
It was more like quarantine camp.
For about a week we stayed there until they checked our papers again.
And they-- the Israelis, at that time the Jews of Palestine came and they checked this out.
In other words, if I was affiliated with Maccabi, that group went to one kibbutz.
If he was affiliated with the [NON-ENGLISH], they went to another kibbutz.
So this is the way I wound up with our 40 people.
We went to a kibbutz, a wonderful, wonderful, lovely place.
No.
Boys and girls.
Boys and girls together.
Well, sadness and joy mixed.
Sadness we're leaving our parents, not knowing if we'll ever see them again.
And joy that we're going to our new life.
I mean, after all, our lives were spared.
We're kids.
And-- and again, we looked forward to Israel.
We-- Palestine at the time.
We were so well-- the-- the sense of Zionism and going to Israel was so well embedded into us.
I'll give you an example.
Just two years ago, there was a 100-year anniversary with the first settlement of Bulgarian that settled in Hartuv.
So there was always that Zionist movement, that wanting to go back to Zion, wanting to go to Israel.

So that was a tremendous desire on our part, through our religious affiliation at home, through our affiliation outside of-- of home, through the school, the affiliation with the Maccabi organization.
So we were always into Judaism and Jewishness and the customs and wanting always go back to-- to-- it was a dream for us always to go back to Palestine.
In the beginning, it was very sketchy.
The first thing was when-- like I said, when my father was restricted from traveling.
Second thing was, like I said to you, the most flagrant thing was when my father's boss came from Germany and he witnessed the persecution there and the Jews being hold to-- to our sidewalks.
And-- and he saw all of that and he was able to come back and not be detained because he enjoyed the privilege of being a former Bulgarian officer.
So as a Jew, he still had certain privileges that he was able to come back.
But there were others that never came back, that they were on trips and they were-- they remained there.
They just closed Germany and that was it.
And we begin to get a real bad feeling after that, because there was the Fascist movement in Bulgaria.
And there was the law in defense of the nation.
And the transition, the change from being-- and I still call that the age of innocence, where everybody I wouldn't say loved each other there, but my god, I played with Turkish kids.
I played with Armenian kids, with Bulgarian kids, with Jewish kids, Greeks.
I mean, we're all in one area.
It was no problem.
We played.
We went to each other's homes.
And all the sudden, that was all taken away.
And it was very, very drastic.
So from there on, all the time, we always aspired to go to Israel.
Yes.
Yes, indeed.
And that was the fear that was creeping through.
Once the-- [DRINKS] there was a sword of Damocles-- Damocles hanging over our heads all the time.
Here the Nazis are right there.
And here the Fascist government is right there.
And the rumors every day are seeping through.
We'll be shipped out.
Well, the rumors were coming through which were really true, because the German ambassador and the German designate for Jewish affairs would come there and-- now, we find out later.
I did not know that.
But they knew that.
The minute they knew that the grown-ups would huddle together.
They say, well, here is-- here we go again.
There is rumor we will be taken away.
There is rumor that we'll be shipped out.
And the biggest fear came after the Jews from Thrace and Macedonia-- when they passed through, we knew we'll be next.
Let me get out of here.
